interactions.summary: Jiang answers what the new order looks like: cheap oil and globalization break, deindustrialization and deurbanization return people to food and manufacturing, nationalism/remilitarization replaces U.S. security, mercantilist blocs emerge, and Japan may adapt better than China because it can transform under existential pressure.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang answers; interviewer asks who wins and loses.

claims.claim: Jiang says the current global economic order rests on cheap oil, and with Hormuz likely closed for years, the world loses the energy basis for globalization.
He forecasts three major trends: deindustrialization/deurbanization toward food and manufacturing, nationalism/remilitarization, and mercantilist self-sufficient spheres.
Jiang says Japan may be better positioned than China because China is overdependent on exports, while Japan has a record of rapid national transformation under threat.
claims.temporal_scope: Winners/losers assessment in the new order.

signature_moments.moment: The supermarket abundance of avocados and cherries becomes Jiang's image of cheap-oil globalization that cannot survive.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a higher standard of living than the Roman emperor
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes the energy basis of everyday abundance concrete.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's expected world after Hormuz/cheap energy breaks: balanced local economies, nationalism, remilitarization, and mercantilist blocs.

chronology_notes.note: This is a concrete dated forecast that cheap-oil globalization is already ending and the 1930s-style bloc world is returning.
